label: Scientists develop DNA origami vaccine platform as stable alternative to mRNA
label_zh: 科學家開發DNA摺紙疫苗平台，作為mRNA的穩定替代方案

timeline.title: Wyss Institute Unveils Stable DNA Origami Vaccine Platform as Scalable mRNA Alternative
timeline.title_zh: 哈佛的 DNA 摺紙疫苗平台效力媲美 mRNA，且穩定性更高、成本更低
timeline.sub_title: Harvard researchers developed 'DoriVac,' a modular nanotechnology using DNA origami to deliver antigens with nanoscale precision, overcoming the storage and manufacturing limitations of current mRNA-lipid nanoparticle vaccines.
timeline.sub_title_zh: 威斯研究所（Wyss Institute）的研究人員研發了 DoriVac，這是一種納米結構疫苗平台，具有分子級精確度及室溫穩定性，為現有的 mRNA 遞送系統提供了一個革命性的替代方案。
timeline.summary:
A multidisciplinary research team led by the Wyss Institute for Biologically Inspired Engineering at Harvard University, in collaboration with the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, has announced a landmark achievement in nanobiotechnology with the unveiling of DoriVac. This new DNA origami-based vaccine platform, described in a study published in Nature Biomedical Engineering, offers a potent and highly stable alternative to messenger RNA (mRNA) technologies. [ref:4] While mRNA vaccines played a pivotal role in curbing the COVID-19 pandemic, they are hindered by manufacturing complexities, the need for extreme cold-chain storage, and variable dosing within lipid nanoparticles. [ref:1, 6] DoriVac seeks to solve these bottlenecks by using DNA as a structural material to create smart vaccines with nanoscale precision.

The core of the DoriVac technology lies in the folding of synthetic DNA into three-dimensional, square block-shaped nanostructures. These [ref:3, 1] blocks act as a sophisticated scaffold where antigens and adjuvants can be mounted with atomic-level control. Researchers [ref:1], including co-corresponding authors William Shih and Girija Goyal, found that the spatial arrangement of these components is critical to vaccine efficacy. By positioning adjuvant molecules—specifically CpG oligonucleotides—at precise intervals of approximately 3.5 nanometers, the team was able to maximize the activation of dendritic cells. These cells are the gatekeepers of the immune system, and their heightened stimulation leads to a cascade of protective responses, including the production of neutralizing antibodies and the mobilization of cytotoxic T cells.

In extensive preclinical testing, the DoriVac platform demonstrated remarkable versatility against a range of deadly pathogens. The researchers targeted the HR2 peptide, a highly conserved region in the spike proteins of SARS-CoV-2, HIV, and Ebola. By focusing [ref:6, 1, 5] on these stable regions, the vaccine could theoretically provide protection across multiple viral variants, moving the world closer to a universal or pan-virus vaccine. In mouse models, the platform triggered a significant increase in antibody-producing B cells and long-lived memory T cells. Furthermore [ref:4], the team utilized the Wyss Institute’s proprietary human lymph node-on-a-chip technology to bridge the gap between animal models and human trials. This microfluidic device allowed them to observe human immune cells responding to the vaccine in a controlled environment, confirming its potential for high efficacy in humans.

One of the most significant advantages of DoriVac over traditional mRNA vaccines is its stability and ease of distribution. Because the [ref:4] platform uses a rigid DNA scaffold rather than fragile mRNA encapsulated in lipid shells, it does not require the ultra-cold temperatures typically associated with current shots. This makes DoriVac a potential game-changer for under-resourced regions where cold-chain infrastructure is lacking. Moreover, the [ref:6] manufacturing process is more streamlined and allows for exact control over the number of molecules per dose. As the team moves toward clinical applications through the startup DoriNano, the platform is being positioned not just for infectious diseases, but also as a tool for personalized cancer immunotherapy, where its ability to orchestrate complex immune signals could redefine how we treat tumors.
timeline.summary_zh:
這是納米生物技術的一個里程碑。哈佛大學維斯研究所（Wyss Institute）與丹娜-法伯癌症研究所（Dana-Farber Cancer Institute）的研究人員正式發佈了 DoriVac，這是一個基於 DNA 摺紙技術（DNA origami）的疫苗平台，有望從根本上重塑全球免疫策略。該研究於 2026 年 3 月 11 日發表在《自然·生物醫學工程》（Nature Biomedical Engineering）雜誌上。該平台解決了現有 mRNA 技術的主要缺陷，特別是在熱穩定性、製造複雜性和劑量精準度方面。研究團隊利用 DNA 的「磚瓦」特性（而非遺傳編碼功能）來構建幾何納米結構，創造出一種自組裝的遞送載體，無需輝瑞（Pfizer）和莫德納（Moderna）疫苗所需的超低溫儲存即可保持穩定。[ref:5, 1, 2, 4, 3]

DoriVac 平台依賴 DNA 摺紙技術將遺傳物質摺疊成微小的方塊狀納米結構。這種設計對疫苗組件的空間排列實現了前所未有的精確控制。在方塊的一側，科學家可以以納米級的精確度附著免疫刺激佐劑分子，而另一側則攜帶來自病原體的特定抗原。這種「納米級間距」對免疫激活至關重要；研究人員發現，精確調整這些分子之間的距離能顯著增強樹突狀細胞（人體主要的抗原呈遞細胞）的反應。與 mRNA 疫苗中使用的脂質納米顆粒（LNP）不同（後者封裝的載荷量往往參差不齊），DoriVac 確保每個顆粒的成分完全一致。[ref:1, 4]

將 DoriVac 與市售 mRNA 疫苗進行對比的臨床前試驗取得了顯著成果。在動物模型和先進的人體器官晶片系統中，DoriVac 誘發了針對 SARS-CoV-2（新冠病毒）、HIV（愛滋病病毒）和伊波拉病毒的免疫反應，其效果與 mRNA-LNP 疫苗相當，在某些情況下甚至更勝一籌。具體而言，在遞送 SARS-CoV-2 刺突蛋白時，該 DNA 摺紙平台觸發了強勁的 B 細胞和 T 細胞激活。研究人員還強調了該平台的「自帶佐劑」特性，這最大限度地減少了對額外化學增強劑的需求，並降低了有時與合成遞送載體相關的脫靶炎症反應風險。[ref:4]

為了驗證該平台在人體使用的潛力，團隊採用了維斯研究所專有的「人體淋巴結晶片」。這種微流控系統讓研究人員能夠在模擬的生物環境中觀察人體免疫細胞如何與納米結構相互作用。研究結果證實，該疫苗能有效招募稀有的 B 細胞並加速初步免疫反應，為單劑接種後獲得更快保護掃清了障礙。這種診斷方法顯著縮短了典型的開發週期，使針對新興病毒變異株的候選疫苗能夠更快速地進行迭代。[ref:1]

這對全球健康公平具有深遠意義。由於 DoriVac 不像 mRNA 疫苗那樣有嚴格的冷鏈要求，它可以在冷藏基礎設施有限的資源匱乏地區更有效地分發。此外，DNA「底盤」的模組化特性允許採用「即插即用」的方法，可以快速更換新抗原以應對不同的疾病或癌症類型。隨著團隊邁向臨床試驗，DoriVac 作為一種高精度、低成本的替代方案，可能最終克服現代疫苗學在結構和物流方面的障礙。

timeline.title: Innovative DNA Origami Vaccine Platform DoriVac Emerges as Stable and High-Precision mRNA Alternative
timeline.title_zh: 創新的DNA摺紙疫苗平台DoriVac，成為穩定且高精準度的mRNA替代方案
timeline.sub_title: Researchers have developed a self-assembling DNA nanostructure platform that offers superior stability and immune cell targeting, potentially overcoming the storage and manufacturing limitations of current mRNA vaccines.
timeline.sub_title_zh: 研究人員開發了一種可自組裝的DNA奈米結構平台，該平台提供卓越的穩定性和免疫細胞靶向能力，有望克服現有mRNA疫苗的儲存和製造限制。
timeline.summary:
In a significant leap for nanobiotechnology, a multidisciplinary team from the Wyss Institute at Harvard University and the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute has unveiled a groundbreaking vaccine platform called DoriVac. Published on March 11, 2026, in Nature Biomedical Engineering, the research introduces a DNA origami-based system that functions as both a vaccine delivery vehicle and an adjuvant. [ref:4, 1, 3] This platform is being positioned as a powerful alternative to messenger RNA (mRNA) technology, offering unprecedented control over the molecular architecture of vaccines while providing much-needed environmental stability.

The DoriVac platform utilizes the principles of DNA origami to fold synthetic DNA strands into highly precise, square block-shaped nanostructures. These [ref:1] structures are engineered with nanometer-level accuracy to display antigens on one face and immune-stimulating adjuvant molecules on the other. This [ref:1, 2] spatial precision allows the vaccine to program immune recognition at a molecular level, a feat that current lipid nanoparticle (LNP) delivery systems for mRNA cannot achieve. By controlling the exact spacing and orientation of these molecules, DoriVac can more effectively trigger the specific receptors on dendritic cells and other immune regulators, leading to a more robust and targeted defense.

One of the most critical advantages of the DoriVac system is its stability. Unlike current mRNA-LNP vaccines, which require stringent cold-chain storage and specialized handling, the DNA origami structures are inherently more durable. Researchers noted that this stability simplifies manufacturing and distribution, making the technology particularly suited for under-resourced regions where maintaining ultra-low temperatures is challenging. The platform also bypasses the complex formulation processes associated with lipid nanoparticles, potentially reducing the costs and technical barriers to entry for global vaccine production.

Preclinical studies involving mouse models and advanced human lymph node-on-a-chip technology have demonstrated the platform's efficacy against a range of pathogens, including SARS-CoV-2, HIV, and Ebola. In direct comparisons with established mRNA vaccines from Pfizer/BioNTech and Moderna, the DNA origami vaccine produced comparable antiviral T-cell and antibody-producing B-cell responses. Furthermore [ref:4], the researchers highlighted that DoriVac triggered a broader activation of the immune system than traditional protein-based scaffolds, which often suffer from "off-target" immune responses where the body attacks the vaccine carrier itself rather than the intended viral antigen.

Looking forward, the modular "plug-and-play" nature of DoriVac suggests it could be rapidly adapted to meet emerging viral threats. The research [ref:1] team is currently exploring the long-term safety of these DNA scaffolds and investigating how different geometric shapes of the origami might further enhance immune memory. As the global health community seeks the next generation of pandemic preparedness tools, DoriVac stands out as a highly customizable, stable, and potent contender to lead the future of immunotherapy and infectious disease prevention.
timeline.summary_zh:
在奈米生物科技領域的重大飛躍中，來自哈佛大學Wyss研究所和Dana-Farber癌症研究所的多學科團隊，揭示了一個名為DoriVac的開創性疫苗平台。該研究於2026年3月11日發表在《Nature Biomedical Engineering》上，介紹了一個基於DNA摺紙的系統，它同時充當疫苗載體和佐劑。該平台被定位為信使核糖核酸（mRNA）技術的強大替代方案，能提供對疫苗分子結構前所未有的控制，同時具備急需的環境穩定性。[ref:4, 1, 3]
DoriVac平台利用DNA摺紙原理，將合成DNA鏈折疊成高度精確的方形塊狀奈米結構。這些結構以奈米級精度進行工程設計，以便在一面上展示抗原，在另一面上展示免疫刺激佐劑分子。這種空間精確性允許疫苗在分子層級上編程免疫識別，這是現有的mRNA脂質奈米顆粒（LNP）遞送系統無法實現的壯舉。透過精確控制這些分子的間距和方向，DoriVac能更有效地觸發樹突細胞和其他免疫調節細胞上的特定受體，從而產生更強大、更具針對性的防禦。[ref:1, 2]
DoriVac系統最關鍵的優勢之一是其穩定性。與目前需要嚴格冷鏈儲存和專業處理的mRNA-LNP疫苗不同，DNA摺紙結構本身更為耐用。研究人員指出，這種穩定性簡化了製造和分發，使該技術特別適合資源匱乏、難以維持超低溫的地區。該平台還繞過了與脂質奈米顆粒相關的複雜配方流程，可能降低了全球疫苗生產的成本和技術門檻。
涉及小鼠模型和先進人類淋巴結晶片技術的臨床前研究，已證明該平台對包括SARS-CoV-2、HIV和Ebola在內的一系列病原體具有療效。與輝瑞/BioNTech和Moderna已建立的mRNA疫苗直接比較，DNA摺紙疫苗產生了相當的抗病毒T細胞和產生抗體的B細胞反應。此外，研究人員強調，DoriVac比傳統的基於蛋白質的支架引發了更廣泛的免疫系統激活，後者常遭受「脫靶」免疫反應，即身體攻擊疫苗載體本身而非目標病毒抗原。[ref:4]
展望未來，DoriVac模組化的「即插即用」特性表明它可以快速適應以應對新出現的病毒威脅。研究團隊目前正在探索這些DNA支架的長期安全性，並研究摺紙的不同幾何形狀如何進一步增強免疫記憶。隨著全球衛生界尋求下一代大流行病防範工具，DoriVac作為一個高度可定制、穩定且強大的競爭者，有望引領未來的免疫療法和傳染病預防。[ref:1]
